title: How over_ratio and under_ratio work
text: Many sampling steps themis accept over_ratio under_ratio argument controls much sampling performed. article explains arguments set .

title: How over_ratio and under_ratio work
text: illustrate, ’ll use small data set known class imbalance. data set, class "" 100 observations, "b" 65, "c" 20.
code: set.seed(1) imbalanced_data <- tibble(   x = rnorm(185),   class = factor(c(rep("a", 100), rep("b", 65), rep("c", 20))) )  count(imbalanced_data, class) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   class     n #>   <fct> <int> #> 1 a       100 #> 2 b        65 #> 3 c        20

title: How over_ratio and under_ratio work
text: over_ratio argument used -sampling steps. controls ratio minority--majority frequencies sampling. target number observations class calculated : target=⌊majority_n×over_ratio⌋\text{target} = \lfloor \text{majority\_n} \times \text{\_ratio} \rfloor majority_n\text{majority\_n} number observations common class. over_ratio = 1 (default): classes upsampled frequency majority class, resulting perfectly balanced data set. over_ratio < 1: Minority classes upsampled fraction majority class frequency, resulting partially balanced data set. over_ratio > 1: classes, including majority, upsampled target, resulting balanced data set larger original. class already least many observations target, left unchanged.

title: How over_ratio and under_ratio work
text: default over_ratio = 1, classes brought 100 observations (size majority class): over_ratio = 0.5, target floor(100 * 0.5) = 50. class "c" upsampled 50. Classes "" "b" already exceed target left unchanged: over_ratio = 0.3, target floor(100 * 0.3) = 30. Class "c" upsampled 20 30. Classes "" "b" exceed target left unchanged:
code: recipe(class ~ x, data = imbalanced_data) |>   step_upsample(class, over_ratio = 1) |>   prep() |>   bake(new_data = NULL) |>   count(class) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   class     n #>   <fct> <int> #> 1 a       100 #> 2 b       100 #> 3 c       100 recipe(class ~ x, data = imbalanced_data) |>   step_upsample(class, over_ratio = 0.5) |>   prep() |>   bake(new_data = NULL) |>   count(class) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   class     n #>   <fct> <int> #> 1 a       100 #> 2 b        65 #> 3 c        50 recipe(class ~ x, data = imbalanced_data) |>   step_upsample(class, over_ratio = 0.3) |>   prep() |>   bake(new_data = NULL) |>   count(class) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   class     n #>   <fct> <int> #> 1 a       100 #> 2 b        65 #> 3 c        30

title: How over_ratio and under_ratio work
text: under_ratio argument used -sampling steps. controls ratio majority--minority frequencies sampling. target number observations class calculated : target=⌊minority_n×under_ratio⌋\text{target} = \lfloor \text{minority\_n} \times \text{\_ratio} \rfloor minority_n\text{minority\_n} number observations least common class. under_ratio = 1 (default): classes downsampled frequency minority class, resulting perfectly balanced data set. under_ratio > 1: Majority classes downsampled multiple minority class frequency, resulting partially balanced data set. under_ratio < 1: classes, including minority, downsampled target, resulting balanced data set smaller original. class already many observations target, left unchanged.

title: How over_ratio and under_ratio work
text: default under_ratio = 1, classes brought 20 observations (size minority class): under_ratio = 2, target floor(20 * 2) = 40. Classes "" "b" downsampled 40. Class "c" already fewer 40 observations left unchanged: under_ratio = 3, target floor(20 * 3) = 60. Classes "" "b" downsampled 60. Class "c" already fewer 60 observations left unchanged:
code: recipe(class ~ x, data = imbalanced_data) |>   step_downsample(class, under_ratio = 1) |>   prep() |>   bake(new_data = NULL) |>   count(class) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   class     n #>   <fct> <int> #> 1 a        20 #> 2 b        20 #> 3 c        20 recipe(class ~ x, data = imbalanced_data) |>   step_downsample(class, under_ratio = 2) |>   prep() |>   bake(new_data = NULL) |>   count(class) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   class     n #>   <fct> <int> #> 1 a        40 #> 2 b        40 #> 3 c        20 recipe(class ~ x, data = imbalanced_data) |>   step_downsample(class, under_ratio = 3) |>   prep() |>   bake(new_data = NULL) |>   count(class) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   class     n #>   <fct> <int> #> 1 a        60 #> 2 b        60 #> 3 c        20

title: How over_ratio and under_ratio work
text: Choosing right ratio depends data model using. default value 1 gives perfectly balanced data set, common choice. However, cases partial balance preferable: Preserving majority class data: perfectly balanced data set -sampling discards majority class data. Using under_ratio > 1 retains data cost less balanced class distribution. Limiting -sampling: large majority class, upsampling perfect balance can generate large amount synthetic data. Using over_ratio < 1 limits amount synthetic data generated. practice, over_ratio under_ratio often treated tunable hyperparameters selected cross-validation. See dials package over_ratio() under_ratio() parameter objects used tuning.

title: Extra Recipes Steps for Dealing with Unbalanced Data
text: Following example using SMOTE algorithm deal unbalanced data
code: library(recipes) library(modeldata) library(themis)  data("credit_data", package = "modeldata")  credit_data0 <- credit_data |>   filter(!is.na(Job))  count(credit_data0, Job) #>         Job    n #> 1     fixed 2805 #> 2 freelance 1024 #> 3    others  171 #> 4   partime  452  ds_rec <- recipe(Job ~ Time + Age + Expenses, data = credit_data0) |>   step_impute_mean(all_predictors()) |>   step_smote(Job, over_ratio = 0.25) |>   prep()  ds_rec |>   bake(new_data = NULL) |>   count(Job) #> # A tibble: 4 × 2 #>   Job           n #>   <fct>     <int> #> 1 fixed      2805 #> 2 freelance  1024 #> 3 others      701 #> 4 partime     701

code: recipe(~., example_data) |>   step_upsample(class, over_ratio = 1) |>   prep() |>   bake(new_data = NULL) |>   ggplot(aes(class)) +   geom_bar() recipe(~., example_data) |>   step_upsample(class, over_ratio = 0.5) |>   prep() |>   bake(new_data = NULL) |>   ggplot(aes(class)) +   geom_bar()

title: Extra Recipes Steps for Dealing with Unbalanced Data
text: following methods share tuning parameter under_ratio, ratio majority--minority frequencies. setting under_ratio = 1 bring number samples majority classes equal 100% minority class.  setting under_ratio = 2 downsample majority class 200% samples minority class 200% samples minority.
code: recipe(~., example_data) |>   step_downsample(class, under_ratio = 1) |>   prep() |>   bake(new_data = NULL) |>   ggplot(aes(class)) +   geom_bar() recipe(~., example_data) |>   step_downsample(class, under_ratio = 2) |>   prep() |>   bake(new_data = NULL) |>   ggplot(aes(class)) +   geom_bar()

title: Condensed Nearest Neighbors — cnn
text: Condensed Nearest Neighbors (CNN) -sampling method reduces majority classes consistent subset: subset classifies original data correctly using 1-nearest-neighbor rule. starts "store" containing minority class observations one randomly chosen majority class observation. repeatedly scans remaining majority class observations moves misclassified 1-nearest neighbor fit current store store. continues full pass adds new observations. observations left outside store removed. smallest class treated minority class always kept. CNN tends keep observations near decision boundary discarding redundant interior observations. seed observation scan order random, results depend random seed. columns used function must numeric missing data.

title: Edited Nearest Neighbors — enn
text: Edited Nearest Neighbors (ENN) cleaning method. observation finds neighbors nearest neighbors , class observation match majority class among neighbors, observation removed. tends remove noisy borderline observations, can lead smoother decision boundaries. Setting times greater 1 applies ENN repeatedly, removing noisy borderline observations pass stopping early pass removes nothing. corresponds Repeated Edited Nearest Neighbors (RENN). Setting all_k = TRUE applies ENN increasing numbers neighbors, 1 neighbors, cleaning data step. corresponds k-Nearest Neighbors (AllKNN) takes precedence times. columns used function must numeric missing data. Use times = Inf repeat ENN convergence.

One "euclidean" (default), "cosine", "mahalanobis", "manhattan", "chebyshev". "euclidean", "cosine", "mahalanobis" use approximate nearest neighbors via RANN package scale well large datasets. "manhattan" "chebyshev" compute exact O(n^2) distance matrix may slow large datasets.
